1:
"And it came to pass a long time after that the LORD had given rest unto Israel from all their enemies round about, that Joshua waxed old and stricken in age." The witnesses note that, a long time after the rest was given, Joshua grew old and near his end. The witnesses note that Gill notes "after many days," that is, years. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Joshua had withdrawn to Timnath-serah to spend his last days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a long time after — the rest given; the rest from enemies — the land at peace; the Joshua waxed old — near his end; the our years a tale — we spend our years as a tale (Ps. 90:9).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens in the time of rest, with the aged leader near his end — "the LORD had given rest unto Israel... Joshua waxed old": the long peace after the wars, and the great age of Joshua, set the scene for his parting counsel; the faithful servant, his work done, prepares to take his leave; it teaches that the days of God's servants are numbered and their labours come to a close, the wise leader using his last strength to charge his people; it bids each remember that "we spend our years as a tale that is told" (Ps. 90:9), Joshua old and the land at rest.

So the witnesses make the verse Joshua waxed old: The witnesses note the a long time after: the rest given. The witnesses note the rest from enemies: the land at peace. The witnesses note the Joshua waxed old: near his end. The witnesses note the our years a tale: we spend our years as a tale (Ps. 90:9). The expositors observe the a long time after: the rest given. The expositors observe the rest from enemies: the land at peace. The expositors observe the Joshua waxed old: near his end. The expositors observe the our years a tale: we spend our years as a tale (Ps. 90:9). The believer is taught to mark the a long time after, the rest from enemies, the Joshua waxed old, the our years a tale, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that, in the time of rest, Joshua grew old — the faithful servant near his end preparing his parting counsel, the days of God's servants numbered, we spend our years as a tale that is told (Ps. 90:9).
2:
"And Joshua called for all Israel, and for their elders, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers, and said unto them, I am old and stricken in age:" The witnesses note Joshua summoning Israel's leaders to give them his parting charge. The witnesses note that Gill notes he summoned them, whether at Timnath-serah, Shechem, or Shiloh. The witnesses note that he addressed the elders, heads, judges, and officers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the called all Israel — the leaders; the elders and heads — judges and officers; the I am old — his last days; the committing to faithful men — commit to faithful men (2 Tim. 2:2).

The witnesses agree Joshua gathered the leaders of the people — "elders... heads... judges... officers" — to lay his last charge upon them: the calling of the heads, that they might carry the word to all, shows the wisdom of charging those who lead the rest; the aged servant owns his weakness, "I am old and stricken in age," yet uses his last days for the people's good; it teaches that the godly leave counsel and testimony to those after them, especially to the leaders who must guide the flock, "the things that thou hast heard of me... commit thou to faithful men" (2 Tim. 2:2), Joshua charging the elders of Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse called all Israel: The witnesses note the called all Israel: the leaders. The witnesses note the elders and heads: judges and officers. The witnesses note the I am old: his last days. The witnesses note the committing to faithful men: commit to faithful men (2 Tim. 2:2). The expositors observe the called all Israel: the leaders. The expositors observe the elders and heads: judges and officers. The expositors observe the I am old: his last days. The expositors observe the committing to faithful men: commit to faithful men (2 Tim. 2:2). The believer is taught to mark the called all Israel, the elders and heads, the I am old, the committing to faithful men, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua summoned the leaders of Israel for his parting charge — the godly leaving counsel to those after, especially the leaders who guide the flock, committing it to faithful men (2 Tim. 2:2).
3:
"And ye have seen all that the LORD your God hath done unto all these nations because of you; for the LORD your God is he that hath fought for you." The witnesses note Joshua reminding them that the LORD himself had fought for them against the nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes how the Lord had destroyed and driven out the seven nations of Canaan to make room for them. The witnesses note that the LORD your God is he that hath fought for you. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye have seen — all the LORD did; the unto these nations — because of you; the the LORD fought — for you; the victories his doing — the LORD shall fight for you (Exod. 14:14).

The witnesses agree Joshua grounds his charge in what their own eyes had seen — "the LORD your God is he that hath fought for you": the whole conquest is ascribed not to their swords but to God's fighting on their behalf; the appeal to their own experience of his power is the surest motive to faithfulness; it teaches that the people of God are to remember that all their victories are his doing, and that the memory of his mighty works should bind them to him, "the LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace" (Exod. 14:14), the LORD himself having fought for Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD fought for you: The witnesses note the ye have seen: all the LORD did. The witnesses note the unto these nations: because of you. The witnesses note the the LORD fought: for you. The witnesses note the victories his doing: the LORD shall fight for you (Exod. 14:14). The expositors observe the ye have seen: all the LORD did. The expositors observe the unto these nations: because of you. The expositors observe the the LORD fought: for you. The expositors observe the victories his doing: the LORD shall fight for you (Exod. 14:14). The believer is taught to mark the ye have seen, the unto these nations, the the LORD fought, the victories his doing, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua reminded them the LORD had fought for them — the conquest ascribed to God's power and not their swords, the memory of his works binding them to him, the LORD fighting for you (Exod. 14:14).
4:
"Behold, I have divided unto you by lot these nations that remain, to be an inheritance for your tribes, from Jordan, with all the nations that I have cut off, even unto the great sea westward." The witnesses note Joshua having divided the land — both the conquered and the unconquered nations — as their inheritance. The witnesses note that Gill notes he divided to them by lot the nations that remained, unsubdued, as well as those conquered. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the nations that remain given as an inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the divided by lot — the nations remaining; the an inheritance — for the tribes; the and those cut off — the conquered; the the title sure — the LORD will give grace and glory (Ps. 84:11).

The witnesses agree Joshua had apportioned to the tribes "these nations that remain... with all the nations that I have cut off": the inheritance was given by promise even where the conquest was not yet complete, the unsubdued land reckoned theirs by God's gift; the dividing of even the remaining nations shows the certainty of the promise that they should yet possess all; it teaches that God's gift to his people is sure though not yet fully enjoyed, the title given before the full possession, "the LORD... will give grace and glory" (Ps. 84:11), the remaining nations divided as their inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse divided by lot: The witnesses note the divided by lot: the nations remaining. The witnesses note the an inheritance: for the tribes. The witnesses note the and those cut off: the conquered. The witnesses note the the title sure: the LORD will give grace and glory (Ps. 84:11). The expositors observe the divided by lot: the nations remaining. The expositors observe the an inheritance: for the tribes. The expositors observe the and those cut off: the conquered. The expositors observe the the title sure: the LORD will give grace and glory (Ps. 84:11). The believer is taught to mark the divided by lot, the an inheritance, the and those cut off, the the title sure, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua had divided even the unconquered nations as their inheritance — God's gift sure though not yet fully enjoyed, the title given before the full possession, the LORD will give grace and glory (Ps. 84:11).
5:
"And the LORD your God, he shall expel them from before you, and drive them from out of your sight; and ye shall possess their land, as the LORD your God hath promised unto you." The witnesses note the promise that the LORD himself would drive out the remaining nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord would expel such of them as yet remained among them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the LORD driving them out and Israel possessing their land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD shall expel — the remaining nations; the drive them out — of your sight; the ye shall possess — their land; the he finishes his work — he will perform it (Phil. 1:6).

The witnesses agree the finishing of the conquest is promised as God's own work — "the LORD your God, he shall expel them... and ye shall possess their land": what remained to be done, God himself would do, that they might enter into the full promise; the assurance that he would complete what he began shows the faithfulness that performs to the end; it teaches that the God who begins his people's deliverance will finish it, driving out every remaining foe in his time, "he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it" (Phil. 1:6), the LORD expelling the nations before them.

So the witnesses make the verse he shall expel them: The witnesses note the the LORD shall expel: the remaining nations. The witnesses note the drive them out: of your sight. The witnesses note the ye shall possess: their land. The witnesses note the he finishes his work: he will perform it (Phil. 1:6). The expositors observe the the LORD shall expel: the remaining nations. The expositors observe the drive them out: of your sight. The expositors observe the ye shall possess: their land. The expositors observe the he finishes his work: he will perform it (Phil. 1:6). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD shall expel, the drive them out, the ye shall possess, the he finishes his work, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the LORD promised to expel the remaining nations himself — the God who begins his people's deliverance finishing it, performing to the end, he which hath begun a good work will perform it (Phil. 1:6).
6:
"Be ye therefore very courageous to keep and to do all that is written in the book of the law of Moses, that ye turn not aside therefrom to the right hand or to the left;" The witnesses note the charge to be very courageous to keep and do all the law, turning aside neither way. The witnesses note that Gill notes to observe the law required spiritual strength, fortitude, holy courage, and firm resolution. The witnesses note that they were to turn not aside to the right hand or the left. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the be very courageous — holy courage; the keep and do — all the law; the written in the book — of Moses; the turn not aside — the law not departing (Josh. 1:8).

The witnesses agree the great charge is to "be very courageous to keep and to do all that is written in the book of the law": Gill notes that obedience itself requires courage — "fortitude of mind, a holy courage, a firm and fixed resolution" — no less than the battles did; the bidding to turn aside "to the right hand or to the left" guards against both excess and defect, all departure from the rule; it teaches that faithfulness to God's word demands a brave and resolute heart, the keeping of the law a thing of holy courage, "this book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth" (Josh. 1:8), to keep all that is written and turn not aside.

So the witnesses make the verse be very courageous: The witnesses note the be very courageous: holy courage. The witnesses note the keep and do: all the law. The witnesses note the written in the book: of Moses. The witnesses note the turn not aside: the law not departing (Josh. 1:8). The expositors observe the be very courageous: holy courage. The expositors observe the keep and do: all the law. The expositors observe the written in the book: of Moses. The expositors observe the turn not aside: the law not departing (Josh. 1:8). The believer is taught to mark the be very courageous, the keep and do, the written in the book, the turn not aside, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua charged them to be very courageous to keep all the law, turning aside neither way — faithfulness to God's word demanding a brave, resolute heart, the book of the law not departing from them (Josh. 1:8).
7:
"That ye come not among these nations, these that remain among you; neither make mention of the name of their gods, nor cause to swear by them, neither serve them, nor bow yourselves unto them:" The witnesses note the charge to keep wholly clear of the nations and their gods. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to converse freely or familiarly with the remaining nations, nor carry on trade with them. The witnesses note that they were not so much as to mention the name of their gods, nor swear by, serve, or bow to them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come not among these nations — no familiarity; the make no mention of their gods — no honour to idols; the nor serve them — nor bow; the be ye separate — come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17).

The witnesses agree the charge of separation is strict and full — "come not among these nations... neither make mention of the name of their gods... nor bow yourselves unto them": the people were to keep clear of idolatry at every point, not even naming the false gods with honour; the thoroughness of the prohibition shows how jealously God guards his people from the contagion of false worship; it teaches that the people of God are to shun every approach to idolatry and ungodly fellowship, keeping themselves unspotted, "wherefore come out from among them, and be ye separate... and touch not the unclean thing" (2 Cor. 6:17), to make no mention of their gods nor serve them.

So the witnesses make the verse come not among them: The witnesses note the come not among these nations: no familiarity. The witnesses note the make no mention of their gods: no honour to idols. The witnesses note the nor serve them: nor bow. The witnesses note the be ye separate: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17). The expositors observe the come not among these nations: no familiarity. The expositors observe the make no mention of their gods: no honour to idols. The expositors observe the nor serve them: nor bow. The expositors observe the be ye separate: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17). The believer is taught to mark the come not among these nations, the make no mention of their gods, the nor serve them, the be ye separate, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua charged them to keep wholly clear of the nations and their gods — shunning every approach to idolatry, keeping themselves unspotted, come out from among them and be ye separate (2 Cor. 6:17).
8:
"But cleave unto the LORD your God, as ye have done unto this day." The witnesses note the heart of the charge — to cleave unto the LORD, as they had done thus far. The witnesses note that Gill notes to cleave to his word, worship, and ordinances externally, and internally by strong affection, hope, trust, and confidence in him. The witnesses note that they had done so unto that day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the cleave unto the LORD — the heart of the charge; the his word and worship — outwardly; the affection and trust — inwardly; the with purpose of heart — cleave unto the Lord (Acts 11:23).

The witnesses agree this verse is the positive heart of the whole charge — "cleave unto the LORD your God": Gill unfolds it as a cleaving both outward, in keeping his word and worship, and inward, in "strong affection, hope, trust, and confidence in him"; the commending of their past faithfulness, "as ye have done unto this day," joins encouragement to exhortation; it teaches that the sum of true religion is to cleave to the LORD with the whole heart, holding fast to him in love and trust, "that with purpose of heart they would cleave unto the Lord" (Acts 11:23), to cleave unto the LORD as they had done.

So the witnesses make the verse cleave unto the LORD: The witnesses note the cleave unto the LORD: the heart of the charge. The witnesses note the his word and worship: outwardly. The witnesses note the affection and trust: inwardly. The witnesses note the with purpose of heart: cleave unto the Lord (Acts 11:23). The expositors observe the cleave unto the LORD: the heart of the charge. The expositors observe the his word and worship: outwardly. The expositors observe the affection and trust: inwardly. The expositors observe the with purpose of heart: cleave unto the Lord (Acts 11:23). The believer is taught to mark the cleave unto the LORD, the his word and worship, the affection and trust, the with purpose of heart, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the heart of the charge was to cleave unto the LORD, as they had done — the sum of true religion holding fast to him in love and trust, that with purpose of heart they cleave unto the Lord (Acts 11:23).
9:
"For the LORD hath driven out from before you great nations and strong: but as for you, no man hath been able to stand before you unto this day." The witnesses note the LORD's driving out great and strong nations, none able to stand before Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes these nations were very numerous and populous, reckoned great and strong in those times. The witnesses note that no man had been able to stand before Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the great nations and strong — driven out; the the LORD did it — not their own strength; the no man could stand — before you; the invincible with God — if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31).

The witnesses agree the LORD's power is magnified in the overthrow of "great nations and strong," before whom "no man hath been able to stand": the conquest of so mighty a people by Israel proves that the strength was not their own but God's; the recalling of their unbroken success encourages continued trust; it teaches that with God on their side the people of God are invincible, the greatest of foes unable to stand against them, "if God be for us, who can be against us?" (Rom. 8:31), no man able to stand before Israel because the LORD drove them out.

So the witnesses make the verse none could stand: The witnesses note the great nations and strong: driven out. The witnesses note the the LORD did it: not their own strength. The witnesses note the no man could stand: before you. The witnesses note the invincible with God: if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31). The expositors observe the great nations and strong: driven out. The expositors observe the the LORD did it: not their own strength. The expositors observe the no man could stand: before you. The expositors observe the invincible with God: if God be for us, who against us (Rom. 8:31). The believer is taught to mark the great nations and strong, the the LORD did it, the no man could stand, the invincible with God, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the LORD drove out great and strong nations, none able to stand before Israel — the strength his and not their own, the people of God invincible with him, if God be for us, who can be against us (Rom. 8:31).
10:
"One man of you shall chase a thousand: for the LORD your God, he it is that fighteth for you, as he hath promised you." The witnesses note the promise that one man should chase a thousand, the LORD fighting for them. The witnesses note that Gill notes some read the future for the past — one had chased a thousand in their war with the Canaanites. The witnesses note that the LORD your God is he that fighteth for you, as he promised. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one shall chase a thousand — the disproportionate victory; the the LORD fighteth — for you; the as he promised — moses' word (Deut. 32:30); the strength in him — the battle is the LORD's (1 Sam. 17:47).

The witnesses agree the secret of Israel's power is again named — "one man of you shall chase a thousand: for the LORD... fighteth for you": the wholly disproportionate victory, one against a thousand, can only be explained by God's own fighting; the promise fulfils the word of Moses, "how should one chase a thousand... except... the LORD had shut them up?" (Deut. 32:30); it teaches that the strength of God's people is wholly in him, that the weakest with God overcomes the mightiest without him, "the battle is the LORD's" (1 Sam. 17:47), one chasing a thousand because the LORD fights for them.

So the witnesses make the verse one chase a thousand: The witnesses note the one shall chase a thousand: the disproportionate victory. The witnesses note the the LORD fighteth: for you. The witnesses note the as he promised: Moses' word (Deut. 32:30). The witnesses note the strength in him: the battle is the LORD's (1 Sam. 17:47). The expositors observe the one shall chase a thousand: the disproportionate victory. The expositors observe the the LORD fighteth: for you. The expositors observe the as he promised: Moses' word (Deut. 32:30). The expositors observe the strength in him: the battle is the LORD's (1 Sam. 17:47). The believer is taught to mark the one shall chase a thousand, the the LORD fighteth, the as he promised, the strength in him, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that one man should chase a thousand, the LORD fighting for them — the strength of God's people wholly in him, the weakest with God overcoming the mightiest, the battle is the LORD's (1 Sam. 17:47; Deut. 32:30).
11:
"Take good heed therefore unto yourselves, that ye love the LORD your God." The witnesses note the charge to take good heed to themselves, that they love the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they needed to be on their guard and watchful, lest they be ensnared by the Canaanites and drawn into idolatry. The witnesses note that they should love the LORD their God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the take good heed — watchfulness; the unto yourselves — self-watch; the that ye love the LORD — the heart kept; the keep the heart — out of it are the issues of life (Prov. 4:23).

The witnesses agree this short verse joins watchfulness to love — "take good heed... that ye love the LORD your God": the keeping of the heart in love to God requires diligent self-watch, lest the affections be drawn away; the warning shows that love to God is to be guarded as the spring of all obedience; it teaches that the people of God must watch over their own hearts, keeping their love to him fervent, for from the heart are the issues of life, "keep thy heart with all diligence; for out of it are the issues of life" (Prov. 4:23), taking good heed to love the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse love the LORD: The witnesses note the take good heed: watchfulness. The witnesses note the unto yourselves: self-watch. The witnesses note the that ye love the LORD: the heart kept. The witnesses note the keep the heart: out of it are the issues of life (Prov. 4:23). The expositors observe the take good heed: watchfulness. The expositors observe the unto yourselves: self-watch. The expositors observe the that ye love the LORD: the heart kept. The expositors observe the keep the heart: out of it are the issues of life (Prov. 4:23). The believer is taught to mark the take good heed, the unto yourselves, the that ye love the LORD, the keep the heart, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua charged them to take good heed that they love the LORD — love to God guarded by diligent self-watch as the spring of all obedience, keeping the heart with all diligence (Prov. 4:23).
12:
"Else if ye do in any wise go back, and cleave unto the remnant of these nations... and shall make marriages with them, and go in unto them, and they to you:" The witnesses note the warning against going back to cleave to the nations and intermarry with them. The witnesses note that Gill notes going back from the Lord and his worship, his word and ordinances. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the soldier who draws back forfeits his life; these were the Lord's soldiers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if ye go back — from the LORD; the cleave to the remnant — of the nations; the make marriages — the door of idolatry; the the unequal yoke — be not unequally yoked (2 Cor. 6:14).

The witnesses agree the great peril is set forth — to "go back... cleave unto the remnant of these nations... and make marriages with them": Clarke marks that the soldier who draws back forfeits his life, and these were the LORD's soldiers; the joining in marriage and fellowship with the heathen was the very door by which idolatry would enter; it teaches that unholy alliances and unequal yokes lead God's people into apostasy, and warns against binding oneself to the ungodly, "be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers" (2 Cor. 6:14), the warning against marriages with the nations.

So the witnesses make the verse go back and intermarry: The witnesses note the if ye go back: from the LORD. The witnesses note the cleave to the remnant: of the nations. The witnesses note the make marriages: the door of idolatry. The witnesses note the the unequal yoke: be not unequally yoked (2 Cor. 6:14). The expositors observe the if ye go back: from the LORD. The expositors observe the cleave to the remnant: of the nations. The expositors observe the make marriages: the door of idolatry. The expositors observe the the unequal yoke: be not unequally yoked (2 Cor. 6:14). The believer is taught to mark the if ye go back, the cleave to the remnant, the make marriages, the the unequal yoke, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua warned against going back to cleave to and intermarry with the nations — unholy alliances the door of apostasy, the danger of the unequal yoke, be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers (2 Cor. 6:14).
13:
"Know for a certainty that the LORD your God will no more drive out any of these nations from before you; but they shall be snares and traps unto you, and scourges in your sides, and thorns in your eyes, until ye perish from off this good land..." The witnesses note the warning that the unexpelled nations would become snares, scourges, and thorns. The witnesses note that Gill notes "knowing ye shall know" — the fullest assurance is given. The witnesses note that they should be snares, traps, scourges in their sides, and thorns in their eyes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no more drive out — if ye go back; the snares and traps — the spared foe; the scourges in your sides — a torment; the thorns in your eyes — vexing you in the land (Num. 33:55).

The witnesses agree the consequence of disobedience is set forth in sharp images — the spared nations would become "snares and traps... scourges in your sides, and thorns in your eyes": what they failed to drive out would turn and afflict them, the unconquered enemy a perpetual torment; the heaping of figures shows how compromise with sin recoils upon the compromiser; it teaches that the sin or enemy God's people spare becomes their scourge, and that incomplete obedience breeds lasting trouble, "they shall be... thorns in your sides... and shall vex you in the land" (Num. 33:55), the nations becoming snares and thorns.

So the witnesses make the verse snares and thorns: The witnesses note the no more drive out: if ye go back. The witnesses note the snares and traps: the spared foe. The witnesses note the scourges in your sides: a torment. The witnesses note the thorns in your eyes: vexing you in the land (Num. 33:55). The expositors observe the no more drive out: if ye go back. The expositors observe the snares and traps: the spared foe. The expositors observe the scourges in your sides: a torment. The expositors observe the thorns in your eyes: vexing you in the land (Num. 33:55). The believer is taught to mark the no more drive out, the snares and traps, the scourges in your sides, the thorns in your eyes, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the unexpelled nations would become snares, scourges, and thorns — compromise with sin recoiling on the compromiser, incomplete obedience breeding lasting trouble, thorns in your sides vexing you in the land (Num. 33:55).
14:
"And, behold, this day I am going the way of all the earth: and ye know in all your hearts and in all your souls, that not one thing hath failed of all the good things which the LORD your God spake concerning you; all are come to pass..." The witnesses note Joshua's great testimony — that not one good thing the LORD promised had failed. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua was about to die, going a journey from this world to another, the way of all the earth. The witnesses note that not one thing had failed of all the good things the LORD promised. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the way of all the earth — joshua near death; the ye know in your hearts — their own witness; the not one thing hath failed — of all the good; the all are come to pass — not one word hath failed (1 Kgs 8:56).

The witnesses agree Joshua, facing death — "going the way of all the earth" — seals his charge with a magnificent testimony to God's faithfulness: "not one thing hath failed of all the good things which the LORD... spake... all are come to pass"; the dying servant calls them to own in their own hearts the perfect keeping of every promise; this is the crown of the chapter, the witness of long experience that God is true to his every word; it teaches that the LORD performs all that he promises, not one good thing falling to the ground, "there hath not failed one word of all his good promise" (1 Kgs 8:56), all the good things come to pass.

So the witnesses make the verse not one thing hath failed: The witnesses note the the way of all the earth: Joshua near death. The witnesses note the ye know in your hearts: their own witness. The witnesses note the not one thing hath failed: of all the good. The witnesses note the all are come to pass: not one word hath failed (1 Kgs 8:56). The expositors observe the the way of all the earth: Joshua near death. The expositors observe the ye know in your hearts: their own witness. The expositors observe the not one thing hath failed: of all the good. The expositors observe the all are come to pass: not one word hath failed (1 Kgs 8:56). The believer is taught to mark the the way of all the earth, the ye know in your hearts, the not one thing hath failed, the all are come to pass, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Joshua, facing death, testified that not one good thing the LORD promised had failed — the crown of his charge, the witness of long experience that God performs his every word, not one word of all his good promise failing (1 Kgs 8:56).
15:
"Therefore it shall come to pass, that as all good things are come upon you, which the LORD your God promised you; so shall the LORD bring upon you all evil things, until he have destroyed you from off this good land..." The witnesses note the warning — that the same faithfulness which brought the good would bring the evil if they transgressed. The witnesses note that Gill notes there was full proof that all the good things promised had come, and it could not be denied. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the threatened bringing of all evil things. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the as all good things came — the proof; the the LORD promised — and performed; the so all evil things — if ye transgress; the faithful in judgment — the LORD will not clear the guilty (Nah. 1:3).

The witnesses agree Joshua turns the proof of God's faithfulness in good into a solemn warning of his faithfulness in judgment — "as all good things are come... so shall the LORD bring upon you all evil things" if they transgress: the God who keeps his promises will as surely keep his threatenings; the certainty of the blessing fulfilled guarantees the certainty of the curse if they apostatize; it teaches that God is as faithful to his warnings as to his promises, and that those who forsake him after such mercies invite a sure judgment, "the LORD will not... clear the guilty" (Nah. 1:3), all evil things to come as the good had come.

So the witnesses make the verse all evil things: The witnesses note the as all good things came: the proof. The witnesses note the the LORD promised: and performed. The witnesses note the so all evil things: if ye transgress. The witnesses note the faithful in judgment: the LORD will not clear the guilty (Nah. 1:3). The expositors observe the as all good things came: the proof. The expositors observe the the LORD promised: and performed. The expositors observe the so all evil things: if ye transgress. The expositors observe the faithful in judgment: the LORD will not clear the guilty (Nah. 1:3). The believer is taught to mark the as all good things came, the the LORD promised, the so all evil things, the faithful in judgment, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that as all the good came, so would all evil if they transgressed — God as faithful to his warnings as his promises, those forsaking him after such mercies inviting sure judgment, the LORD who will not clear the guilty (Nah. 1:3).
16:
"When ye have transgressed the covenant of the LORD your God... and have gone and served other gods... then shall the anger of the LORD be kindled against you, and ye shall perish quickly from off the good land which he hath given unto you." The witnesses note the final warning — that transgressing the covenant and serving other gods would bring swift ruin. The witnesses note that Gill notes the transgressing of the covenant of the Lord. The witnesses note that Clarke cites Trapp: this judgment Joshua inculcates again and again. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the transgressed the covenant — by idolatry; the served other gods — the apostasy; the the anger kindled — against them; the perish from the land — if ye forsake him, he will forsake you (2 Chr. 15:2).

The witnesses agree the charge closes with the sharpest warning — "when ye have transgressed the covenant... and served other gods... ye shall perish quickly from off the good land": the breaking of the covenant by idolatry would kindle God's anger and bring swift destruction; Clarke, citing Trapp, notes how Joshua "inculcates" this judgment again and again, that it might sink deep; it teaches that apostasy from God is the sure road to ruin, and that the good land itself is no safeguard to those who forsake the LORD, "if ye forsake him, he will forsake you" (2 Chr. 15:2); thus Joshua's first farewell ends, warning that transgression brings swift perishing from the good land.

So the witnesses make the verse perish quickly: The witnesses note the transgressed the covenant: by idolatry. The witnesses note the served other gods: the apostasy. The witnesses note the the anger kindled: against them. The witnesses note the perish from the land: if ye forsake him, he will forsake you (2 Chr. 15:2). The expositors observe the transgressed the covenant: by idolatry. The expositors observe the served other gods: the apostasy. The expositors observe the the anger kindled: against them. The expositors observe the perish from the land: if ye forsake him, he will forsake you (2 Chr. 15:2). The believer is taught to mark the transgressed the covenant, the served other gods, the the anger kindled, the perish from the land, and to cleave unto the LORD his God. The verse thus records that transgressing the covenant and serving other gods would bring swift ruin — apostasy the sure road to destruction, the good land no safeguard to those who forsake the LORD, if ye forsake him, he will forsake you (2 Chr. 15:2).
